did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) deep learning model to classify dermoscopic skin images into benign, malignant, and normal categories, achieving 84.14% accuracy on the Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) on the Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset. It includes specific methodology sections (data preprocessing, model architecture, training parameters like batch size and epochs), reports original results (84.14% accuracy, class-specific F1 scores), and discusses these findings. Although published in a conference proceedings (AICDMB), it contains full original research content rather than being a survey or review.

title: Skin Cancer Classification Using Deep Learning Techniques
journal: 2025 Annual International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning and Blockchain Technology (AICDMB)
brief_summary: This study developed a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify dermoscopic skin images into benign, malignant, and normal categories using the Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset. The methodology involved preprocessing images to 224x224 pixels, applying data augmentation, and training the model for 50 epochs with a batch size of 32. The system achieved an overall accuracy of 84.14%, with class-specific F1 scores of 0.95 for normal, 0.85 for benign, and 0.78 for malignant cases. Evaluation metrics also included a Positive Predictive Value of 81.67% and sensitivity of 83.45%. The results demonstrate the model's potential as a reliable support tool for dermatologists in early skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classification categories used in the experiment on the Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset. The model achieved an F1 score of 0.85 for this class.
One of the three classification categories used in the experiment. The paper notes melanoma as the most severe form of skin cancer. The model achieved an F1 score of 0.78 for this class.
One of the three classification categories used in the experiment, sourced from the 'Normal Skin Dataset' on Kaggle. The model achieved an F1 score of 0.95 for this class.

all_task_types.target: Dermoscopic skin images classified into three discrete categories: benign, malignant, and normal.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin image classification' using a custom CNN. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign, malignant, normal) using a softmax output layer. It reports class-specific F1 scores (0.95 for normal, 0.85 for benign, 0.78 for malignant) and overall accuracy (84.14%), which are standard metrics for multi-class classification. The loss function used was categorical cross-entropy, confirming a classification task.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy (84.14%), Positive Predictive Value (81.67%), sensitivity (83.45%), and F1-scores.

Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset (Malignant vs.notes: The model was trained for 50 epochs with a batch size of 32. The confusion matrix indicated high true positives and few misclassifications. Training and validation accuracy graphs showed steady improvement with a small gap indicating good generalization without overfitting. Training loss decreased steadily, while validation loss decreased at a slightly slower rate.

conclusions: The proposed custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) system achieved an overall accuracy of 84.14% in classifying dermoscopic skin images into benign, malignant, and normal categories.
The model demonstrated class-specific F1 scores of 0.95 for normal skin, 0.85 for benign lesions, and 0.78 for malignant lesions.
The system achieved a Positive Predictive Value (PPV) of 81.67% and a sensitivity of 83.45%.

The study concludes that the proposed deep learning system has significant potential to assist dermatologists in the early detection of skin cancer by reducing diagnostic errors and enabling quicker, more accurate decisions.
The authors acknowledge that the system's generalization could be improved by incorporating larger and more diverse datasets across different demographics.

Future work is suggested to explore multi-class classification for distinguishing various skin cancer types, integrating real-time detection capabilities, and utilizing advanced techniques like transfer learning to further improve accuracy and clinical utility.
important_remarks: The study relies on public Kaggle datasets (Malignant vs Benign and Normal Skin) rather than a large-scale, multi-center clinical dataset, which limits the generalizability of the results to diverse real-world demographics.

The model achieved an accuracy of 84.14%, which is moderate compared to state-of-the-art deep learning models in dermatology, and showed lower performance for malignant lesions (F1 score 0.78) compared to normal (0.95) and benign (0.85) classes.

The paper does not report the specific number of images used for training, validation, and testing, nor the class distribution, making it impossible to assess potential class imbalance or data leakage risks.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal test split of the Kaggle dataset, raising concerns about overfitting to specific dataset characteristics.

The CNN architecture is described as 'custom' but lacks detailed hyperparameter specifications (e.g., number of filters, layer depth, dropout rates) beyond kernel size and activation functions, hindering reproducibility.

The paper acknowledges that generalization could be improved by incorporating larger and more diverse datasets, indicating current limitations in robustness across different skin types and lesion presentations.

The study is published in a conference proceedings (AICDMB) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny of the methodology and results compared to high-impact medical AI journals.
